# Values and Culture {#sec-values}

## Lab values

Our work depends on trust, rigor, and mutual respect. These values guide how we interact with each other, our collaborators, and the broader research community.

### Scientific integrity

We prioritize getting it right over getting it published. This means pre-specifying analyses where appropriate, reporting null results honestly, stress-testing our own findings, and welcoming scrutiny from others.

### Reproducibility

Every result we produce should be reproducible by someone else using our code and data. This is not just good practice — it is a responsibility to the communities whose policy decisions may depend on our work.

### Collaboration over competition

We succeed as a group. Lab members are encouraged to share knowledge freely, help each other debug code, co-author across projects, and celebrate each other's wins.

### Inclusion and respect

We are committed to building a lab where people of all backgrounds feel welcome and valued. Diverse perspectives make our science better.

### Sustainability

We work on environmental problems, and we should be mindful of our own footprint — from the energy cost of computation to the carbon cost of travel.

## Lab culture

### Communication norms

- **Slack** is the primary channel for day-to-day communication. Use it liberally — asking questions is always encouraged.
- **Email** is for formal or external communications. For internal conversations, prefer Slack.
- Respond to messages within one business day when possible. If you need more time, acknowledge receipt and give a timeline.

### Work–life balance

Research is a marathon, not a sprint. We do not expect or encourage working nights or weekends as a norm. If you find yourself consistently overloaded, talk to Tamma or the lab manager — we will find a solution together.

### Intellectual generosity

When someone shares an idea, a draft, or a piece of code, engage with it constructively. Lead with what works before suggesting changes. Give credit generously.
